channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: What Tuesday’s primaries say about the future of the Democratic Party
item.description: Democratic socialist candidates won key races in New York, while more establishment candidates prevailed in other contests.

item.title: Primary results. War powers. Reflecting Pool. ICE protesters. AI bias test. College credits. Perfect bedtime. Catch up now.
item.description: The 7.

item.title: Rep. April McClain Delaney (D-Maryland) fends off wine mogul David Trone’s comeback bid after a bitter primary battle with millions spent
item.description: The acrimonious campaign marked a comeback bid for Trone, who represented Maryland’s western-most congressional district for six years before losing his $60 million self-financed bid for the Senate two years ago.

item.title: All 3 Mamdani-backed candidates win U.S. House primaries in New York City. An earlier alert misstated how many incumbents had lost.
item.description: Brad Lander defeated two-term incumbent Rep. Dan Goldman in New York’s 10th District, while Claire Valdez won the Democratic primary to succeed Rep. Nydia Velázquez in the 7th District. In the 13th District, Darializa Avila Chevalier, a doctoral student and Mamdani campaign organizer, unseated Rep. Adriano Espaillat.

item.title: Dan Cox wins primary and will challenge Maryland Gov. Wes Moore. The MAGA-aligned Republican lost soundly to Moore in 2022.
item.description: Moore, running for a second term, is a rising star in the Democratic Party and a possible 2028 presidential contender.

item.title: State assemblyman Micah Lasher projected to win Democratic primary in New York to replace Rep. Jerrold Nadler
item.description: The Democratic field drew early national attention for attracting Jack Schlossberg, the grandson of President John F. Kennedy, and George Conway, a former Republican lawyer who had previously been married to Trump adviser Kellyanne Conway.

item.title: Adrian Boafo wins Democratic primary in Maryland to replace Rep. Steny Hoyer after a deluge of super PAC spending on his behalf
item.description: Hoyer’s retirement touched off a six-month campaign sprint that drew 24 candidates and nearly $11 million tied to cryptocurrency and pro-Israel interests.

item.title: Two Mamdani-backed candidates win U.S. House primaries in New York City to unseat incumbents, in a test of leftist politics
item.description: Brad Lander defeated two-term incumbent Rep. Dan Goldman in New York’s 10th District, while Claire Valdez won the Democratic primary to succeed Rep. Nydia Velázquez in the 7th District. A third Mamdani-backed candidate was still locked in a tight race, but the victories served as a testament to the growing democratic socialist movement.

item.title: Los Angeles Unified School District passes strict new screen time rules for students
item.description: The limits for students in the nation’s second-largest public school district are some of the most prescriptive and slated to be phased in starting in August, sooner than policies set by other districts. No screen time will be permitted for children until they’re in second grade, with progressively looser restrictions thereafter.

item.title: Wizards select 19-year-old BYU star AJ Dybantsa with top overall pick in NBA draft
item.description: The 6-foot-9 wing, who is widely considered a potential franchise cornerstone and whose 25.5 points per game led the nation in his only college season, helps transform Washington’s outlook from recent sub-20-win campaigns to a team closer to competitive expectations.

item.title: South Carolina Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette, Trump’s original pick for governor, loses to Attorney General Alan Wilson in primary runoff
item.description: The president endorsed Evette in the primary but hedged his bets Friday by endorsing both Evette and Wilson in the GOP runoff. “It’s a Wealth of Riches — With either one you can’t go wrong,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

item.title: Congress passes housing help for Americans in rare act of bipartisanship
item.description: The House approved the legislation in a 358-32 vote, sending it to President Donald Trump’s desk. With housing costs ranking among voters’ top economic concerns, Democrats and Republicans found common ground on a normally partisan issue that allows both parties to show they are taking action on affordability as midterms loom.

item.title: The advice about rooftop solar panels is changing. Do this instead.
item.description: The end of federal tax credits for rooftop solar and flexible new subscriptions have changed the pros and cons of leasing versus buying.

item.title: What Brexit really gave Britain
item.description: Leaving the European Union has made British politicians more accountable for the U.K.’s problems, which persist.

item.title: Senate votes to block Trump from resuming Iran war
item.description: The Senate passed the first war powers resolution since the start of the conflict, but Republicans argue it does not have the force of law. The measure passed the House earlier this month.

item.title: Members of alleged antifa group in Texas get decades-long sentences for violent protest outside ICE facility
item.description: Eight people got prison terms ranging from 30 to 100 years for their roles in a protest that turned violent outside an immigration detention center last summer.

item.title: Want a younger brain? Exercise in your 40s may help.
item.description: Even if you’ve never worked out, starting in middle age — even with brisk walks — can have a bigger impact on your brain than you think.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Do they host two grandkids when it means always excluding a third?
item.description: Grandparents respect their three kids’ individual parenting styles, but one grandson is kept home from special cousins’ trips.

item.title: The CDC’s chief blocked a study that found benefits to covid vaccines. Now it’s in a top medical journal.
item.description: The study found that the vaccine reduced the risk of emergency department visits and hospitalizations among healthy adults by about half last winter. Jay Bhattacharya, the CDC’s interim director, had concerns about the methods used to calculate vaccine effectiveness, an HHS spokesman said in April.

item.title: What’s America really like? Ask the World Cup tourists.
item.description: Foreign soccer fans marvel at features of U.S. life we take for granted.

item.title: Supreme Court says Rastafarian can’t sue prison officials over shorn dreadlocks, a departure from recent religious freedom rulings
item.description: The justices found Damon Landor could not individually sue prison officials who forcibly sheared his dreadlocks under a 2000 federal law that requires states to protect the religious rights of prisoners in state institutions.

item.title: Nasdaq tumbles more than 2% in opening trading as global tech sell-off deepens
item.description: Global stocks fell sharply and appeared poised to extend losses in a sell-off driven by tech heavyweights.

item.title: A music legend in today’s On the Record
item.description: A music legend in today’s On the Record

item.title: Justice Department issued, then withdrew subpoenas to force Post, WSJ reporters to testify
item.description: Subpoenas for reporters to appear before a grand jury are exceedingly rare. The journalists have not testified.

item.title: Iran talks. Kennedy Center. Murder conviction. Election bets. Heat wave. Brain aging. Fried apple pie. Catch up now.
item.description: The 7.

item.title: A sleep doctor shares signs that you could have a real sleep problem
item.description: If you’re tired all the time, you might not just be bad at sleeping. You could actually have a medical condition.

item.title: New photos of Kennedy Center show first look at facade without Trump’s name
item.description: The images were taken from inside the tarp-covered scaffolding that has blocked the public’s view for more than a week.

item.title: Why Trump has been attacking the Supreme Court, with 3 key rulings ahead
item.description: As the court prepares to rule on several of the president’s priorities, tensions are running high — even with his own appointees.

item.title: GLP-1s and MAHA are driving a seismic shift in America’s snacking
item.description: Cottage cheese is in and chips are out as Americans buy more high-protein foods and fewer processed snacks.

item.title: Federal judge rejects Justice Department subpoenas issued to Minnesota governor and Minneapolis mayor over immigration enforcement
item.description: The decision by Minnesota’s chief federal judge marked another defeat in the Trump administration’s attempts to pressure Democratic leaders to help carry out the president’s aggressive immigration agenda.

item.title: ABC starts running TV spots urging viewers to push back against FCC pressure
item.description: The federal regulator is investigating the Disney-owned television company on two fronts, with “The View” and ABC’s local stations targeted.

item.title: Clive Davis dies at 94. The music executive guided the careers of stars including Whitney Houston and Bob Dylan.
item.description: At Columbia Records and then Arista, he became one of the most powerful executives in the recording industry.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Does her running group buddy have a secret drinking problem?
item.description: Her running buddy reeks of alcohol every morning, and she doesn’t know whether to speak up or if it’s none of her business.

item.title: These ‘city killers’ threaten civilization. Hunt them down.
item.description: Investing in interplanetary defense isn’t expensive, and it could save the planet.

item.title: Vance says Iran agrees to nuclear inspections, as under Obama-era deal that Trump threw out
item.description: Conversations with inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency could occur as soon as Monday, the vice president said, as negotiations continue in Switzerland.

item.title: A blockbuster in today’s On the Record
item.description: Take today’s news quiz to earn your Monday points.

item.title: Alan Greenspan, most powerful central banker of modern times, dies at 100
item.description: His nearly two-decade run as chairman of the Federal Reserve helped spur prosperity, but his decisions also contributed to the 2008 financial crisis.

item.title: Iran talks. Keir Starmer. Reflecting Pool. Voter turnout. Eating habits. Sleep disorders. Dad jokes. Catch up now.
item.description: The 7.

item.title: U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigns as Labour government seeks reboot
item.description: The party's crushing losses in nationwide local elections triggered a mutiny against Starmer, who has struggled to define his agenda and deliver on campaign promises.

item.title: We asked for your best dad jokes. Here are 39 to delight and annoy you.
item.description: Yes, they are corny. But dad jokes are also a way for people — even those who aren’t actual fathers — to connect and build social bonds.

item.title: The latest data on baby names reveals a progressive paradox
item.description: The most progressive parents in the United States are giving their children the most traditional names.

item.title: What a sleep doctor says parents should know before giving melatonin to their children
item.description: It’s the most widely studied sleep aid in children, but many parents still feel uncertain or even guilty about using it despite clear benefits.

item.title: World Cup visitors marvel at American food, from cheesesteaks to cheese slices
item.description: For attendees who have plunked down thousands on travel, not to mention the most expensive tickets in World Cup history, cheap (and greasy) eats are a way to save a buck and take in U.S. culture.

item.title: Trump faces new hurdles after Iran ceasefire: Tehran’s leverage, Israel’s attacks, MAGA’s backlash
item.description: The truce reopened diplomacy, but it left unresolved the question at the heart of the conflict: what limits, if any, Tehran will accept on its nuclear program. Vice President JD Vance is meeting Sunday with senior Iranian leaders in an effort to chart a path for broader peace.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Brother’s anger is terrifying. Now his wife wants support.
item.description: Carolyn Hax: Brother’s anger is terrifying. Now his wife wants support.

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Play now to finish out your score for the week.

item.title: Confidential files contained directives for Tulsi Gabbard. Did they come from her guru?
item.description: A Post reporter obtained hundreds of confidential memos detailing politics and policy guidance for Gabbard from her years in Congress, then embarked on a quest to identify who was behind them.

item.title: What retirement revealed about my father
item.description: I blamed him for what he couldn’t fix. Now I see he’s extraordinary.

item.title: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now
item.description: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now

item.title: Trump says he is likely to drain Reflecting Pool again, citing need for repairs after $14 million renovation
item.description: The president’s renovation of the pool led to a resurgence of algae and then peeling paint, which was observed as workers attempted to clear it.
